title: Travel care service at Gatwick Airport for travelers with problems
transcript: going to hear a radio program. You will hear the program twice. First read items 46-55. After you've listened to the program, decide if the statement for each of the items 46-55 is true or not true. Mark your answer on the answer sheet. Now you have one minute to read the statements. You're going to hear a report about Travel Care, a service that Gatwick Airport offers for travellers with problems. Ruth London, a Travel Care employee, will be telling the reporter about her work and some of the people she has helped. It's a weekday morning at Gatwick Airport. In the offices of Travel Care, the airport's emergency service for travellers, the week's first case is fast asleep. For the past three days, Tommaso, an 18-year-old Italian, has been trying to avoid the Gatwick Airport police, sleeping wherever he can before being moved on. Ruth London, a former British Airways stewardess, one of three part-time advisors employed by Travel Care, would like to do more to help, but Tommaso hasn't made it easy for her. He came here on a one-way flight, hoping some relatives in Wales would look after him, but they don't want him. Since then, I can only suppose he's been sleeping rough. It was Ruth who helped him when he first arrived at Gatwick last month, without a penny to his name. When she requested assistance from the Italian Embassy, she was told they had given Tommaso a return train ticket back to Italy some weeks before. Unfortunately, he didn't speak English, so we employed the services of a young Italian woman who works as a waitress at the Gatwick coffee shop. Through her, we discovered that he had torn up his train ticket, but we couldn't find out why. And when, finally, the local Italian police made contact with his mother, the answer came back that she doesn't want to know him either. Last year, 23 million people visited Gatwick, and 2,000 of them ended up being helped by Travel Care. Among them was Darren Tyler, a 23-year-old student from Belfast. Darren arrived here just over a fortnight ago on the promise of a job in London, but when he got there, the family friend who had guaranteed him the work had already given the job to his own son. Darren stayed with the family for two weeks, but the relationship soon got difficult, and one night he turned up here at Gatwick, after his mother told him she had booked him onto a return flight. When he got here, he found out that his mum hadn't booked him on the flight after all, so he'd been sleeping in the airport. Like most Gatwick passengers, Darren had never heard of Travel Care. The service does not advertise its presence. They have learned from past experience and no longer consider themselves to be an easy option for people trying to get a cheap holiday. Some people do try, thinking we'll give them money on any old excuse, but I never give them the cash. I always go to the ticket office with them. If they're young or are not particularly helpful about who we could contact, we show them where the motorway is. We'll give them sandwiches and something to drink to prepare them for the journey, but it's amazing how often fathers will pay the money for tickets once Travel Care has got them on the phone. Britain leads the world in emergency help for travellers. Apart from a similar organisation at London's other airport, Heathrow, no other airport in the world offers such a service. Travel Care is financed by Gatwick Airport Limited, British Airways and the British Government. They also receive money from private people, some of them grateful for help they themselves have received. This money pays for the three employees and twelve other assistants, most of them former airport staff, ranging from airport directors to duty-free shop assistants. But in spite of all the facilities they have here at Travel Care, there are a few travellers who simply cannot be helped. You get some people who are very religious and they turn up at the airport believing God will help. One woman I remember told us that she didn't need any help because God would find a way for her to fly to America to see her son. She waited at Gatwick for two days, but no help came. All of which, I suppose, just goes to prove that God does not provide airline tickets. Now listen to the radio programme again. It's a weekday morning at Gatwick Airport. In the offices of Travel Care, the airport's emergency service for travellers, the week's first case is fast asleep. For the past three days, Tommaso, an 18-year-old Italian, has been trying to avoid the Gatwick Airport police, sleeping wherever he can before being moved on. Ruth London, a former British Airways stewardess, one of three part-time advisors employed by Travel Care, would like to do more to help, but Tommaso hasn't made it easy for her. He came here on a one-way flight, hoping some relatives in Wales would look after him, but they don't want him. Since then, I can only suppose he's been sleeping rough. It was Ruth who helped him when he first arrived at Gatwick last month, without a penny to his name. When she requested assistance from the Italian embassy, she was told they had given Tommaso a return train ticket back to Italy some weeks before. Unfortunately, he didn't speak English, so we employed the services of a young Italian woman who works as a waitress at the Gatwick coffee shop. Through her, we discovered that he had torn up his train ticket, but we couldn't find out why. And when, finally, the local Italian police made contact with his mother, the answer came back that she doesn't want to know him either. Last year, 23 million people visited Gatwick, and 2,000 of them ended up being helped by Travel Care. Among them was Darren Tyler, a 23-year-old student from Belfast. Darren arrived here just over a fortnight ago on the promise of a job in London, but when he got there, the family friend who had guaranteed him the work had already given the job to his own son. Darren stayed with the family for two weeks, but the relationship soon got difficult, and one night he turned up here at Gatwick after his mother told him she had booked him onto a return flight. When he got here, he found out that his mum hadn't booked him on the flight after all, so he'd been sleeping in the airport. Like most Gatwick passengers, Darren had never heard of Travel Care. The service does not advertise its presence. They have learned from past experience and no longer consider themselves to be an easy option for people trying to get a cheap holiday. Some people do try, thinking we'll give them money on any old excuse, but I never give them the cash. I always go to the ticket office with them. If they're young or are not particularly helpful about who we could contact, we show them where the motorway is. We'll give them sandwiches and something to drink to prepare them for the journey, but it's amazing how often fathers will pay the money for tickets once Travel Care has got them on the phone. Britain leads the world in emergency help for travellers. Apart from a similar organisation at London's other airport, Heathrow, no other airport in the world offers such a service. Travel Care is financed by Gatwick Airport Ltd, British Airways and the British Government. They also receive money from private people, some of them grateful for help they themselves have received. This money pays for the three employees and twelve other assistants, most of them former airport staff, ranging from airport directors to duty-free shop assistants. But in spite of all the facilities they have here at Travel Care, there are a few travellers who simply cannot be helped. You get some people who are very religious and they turn up at the airport believing God will help. One woman I remember told us that she didn't need any help because God would find a way for her to fly to America to see her son. She waited at Gatwick for two days but no help came. All of which I suppose just goes to prove that God does not provide airline tickets. Now you have one minute to check your answers again. www.gatwickairport.co.uk www.gatwickairport.co.uk

statements.statement: Travel-Care is a travel organisation giving passengers information about their flights.
statements.audio_transcript: It's a weekday morning at Gatwick Airport. In the offices of Travel Care, the airport's emergency service for travellers, the week's first case is fast asleep.

explanation.de: FALSCH. Travel Care wird als «Notfalldienst des Flughafens für Reisende» beschrieben, nicht als Fluginformationsstelle. Es hilft Menschen in Notsituationen, gibt aber keine Fluginformationen.
explanation.en: INCORRECT. Travel Care is described as 'the airport's emergency service for travellers', not a flight information service. It helps people in crisis situations, not with flight details.
explanation.ar: خطأ. يُوصف Travel Care بأنه 'خدمة الطوارئ في المطار للمسافرين' وليس خدمة معلومات الرحلات. إنه يساعد الناس في حالات الطوارئ.
explanation.fr: FAUX. Travel Care est décrit comme 'le service d'urgence de l'aéroport pour les voyageurs', pas comme un service d'information sur les vols. Il aide les personnes en difficulté.
explanation.es: INCORRECTO. Travel Care se describe como 'el servicio de emergencia del aeropuerto para viajeros', no como un servicio de información de vuelos. Ayuda a personas en situaciones de crisis.
explanation.ru: НЕПРАВИЛЬНО. Travel Care описывается как 'служба экстренной помощи аэропорта для путешественников', а не служба информации о рейсах.

statements.statement: Ruth London used to be a stewardess.
statements.audio_transcript: Ruth London, a former British Airways stewardess, one of three part-time advisors employed by Travel Care, would like to do more to help, but Tommaso hasn't made it easy for her.

explanation.de: RICHTIG. Im Audio wird Ruth London als «ehemalige British Airways Stewardess» beschrieben. Sie war also früher Flugbegleiterin.
explanation.en: CORRECT. The audio describes Ruth London as 'a former British Airways stewardess'. She used to work as a flight attendant before joining Travel Care.
explanation.ar: صحيح. يصف التسجيل روث لندن بأنها 'مضيفة سابقة في الخطوط الجوية البريطانية'. كانت تعمل كمضيفة طيران سابقاً.
explanation.fr: CORRECT. L'audio décrit Ruth London comme 'une ancienne hôtesse de l'air de British Airways'. Elle était autrefois hôtesse de l'air.
explanation.es: CORRECTO. El audio describe a Ruth London como 'una ex azafata de British Airways'. Antes trabajaba como auxiliar de vuelo.
explanation.ru: ПРАВИЛЬНО. В аудио Рут Лондон описывается как 'бывшая стюардесса British Airways'. Раньше она работала бортпроводницей.

statements.statement: Tomasso flew to Great Britain because he wanted to move in with members of his family living in Wales.
statements.audio_transcript: He came here on a one-way flight, hoping some relatives in Wales would look after him, but they don't want him.

explanation.de: RICHTIG. Im Audio heißt es, Tommaso sei «mit einem Einwegflug gekommen, in der Hoffnung, dass Verwandte in Wales sich um ihn kümmern» würden. Er wollte also bei seiner Familie in Wales leben.
explanation.en: CORRECT. The audio says he 'came here on a one-way flight, hoping some relatives in Wales would look after him'. He flew to Britain to live with family members in Wales.
explanation.ar: صحيح. يقول التسجيل إنه 'جاء برحلة ذهاب فقط، على أمل أن يعتني به أقارب في ويلز'. سافر إلى بريطانيا للعيش مع عائلته.
explanation.fr: CORRECT. L'audio dit qu'il 'est venu avec un vol aller simple, espérant que des proches au Pays de Galles s'occuperaient de lui'. Il a volé en Grande-Bretagne pour vivre avec sa famille.
explanation.es: CORRECTO. El audio dice que 'vino con un vuelo de ida, esperando que unos parientes en Gales cuidaran de él'. Voló a Gran Bretaña para vivir con miembros de su familia.
explanation.ru: ПРАВИЛЬНО. В аудио говорится, что он 'прилетел в одну сторону, надеясь, что родственники в Уэльсе позаботятся о нём'. Он прилетел в Британию, чтобы жить с семьёй.

statements.statement: The Italian government has given Tomasso a ticket to return to Italy.
statements.audio_transcript: When she requested assistance from the Italian Embassy, she was told they had given Tommaso a return train ticket back to Italy some weeks before.

explanation.de: RICHTIG. Im Audio heißt es, die italienische Botschaft habe Tommaso «einige Wochen zuvor eine Rückfahrkarte nach Italien» gegeben. Die Botschaft vertritt die Regierung.
explanation.en: CORRECT. The audio says the Italian Embassy 'had given Tommaso a return train ticket back to Italy some weeks before'. The embassy represents the Italian government.
explanation.ar: صحيح. يقول التسجيل إن السفارة الإيطالية 'أعطت توماسو تذكرة قطار عودة إلى إيطاليا قبل بضعة أسابيع'. السفارة تمثل الحكومة.
explanation.fr: CORRECT. L'audio dit que l'ambassade italienne 'avait donné à Tommaso un billet de train retour pour l'Italie quelques semaines auparavant'. L'ambassade représente le gouvernement.
explanation.es: CORRECTO. El audio dice que la embajada italiana 'le había dado a Tommaso un billete de tren de regreso a Italia semanas antes'. La embajada representa al gobierno.
explanation.ru: ПРАВИЛЬНО. В аудио говорится, что итальянское посольство 'дало Томмасо обратный билет на поезд в Италию несколько недель назад'. Посольство представляет правительство.

statements.statement: Tomasso and Daren Tylor are just two of more than 20 million people who receive help from Travel-Care each year.
statements.audio_transcript: Last year, 23 million people visited Gatwick, and 2,000 of them ended up being helped by Travel Care.

explanation.de: FALSCH. Die Aussage verwechselt die Zahlen. Im Audio heißt es, 23 Millionen Menschen besuchten Gatwick, aber nur «2.000 von ihnen» erhielten Hilfe von Travel Care. Nicht 20 Millionen werden geholfen.
explanation.en: INCORRECT. The statement confuses the numbers. The audio says '23 million people visited Gatwick, and 2,000 of them ended up being helped by Travel Care'. Only 2,000 people receive help, not 20 million.
explanation.ar: خطأ. تخلط العبارة بين الأرقام. يقول التسجيل '23 مليون شخص زاروا غاتويك، و2000 منهم تلقوا المساعدة'. فقط 2000 شخص يتلقون المساعدة.
explanation.fr: FAUX. L'énoncé confond les chiffres. L'audio dit '23 millions de personnes ont visité Gatwick, et 2 000 d'entre elles ont été aidées par Travel Care'. Seules 2 000 personnes reçoivent de l'aide.
explanation.es: INCORRECTO. La afirmación confunde los números. El audio dice '23 millones de personas visitaron Gatwick, y 2.000 de ellas recibieron ayuda de Travel Care'. Solo 2.000 personas reciben ayuda, no 20 millones.
explanation.ru: НЕПРАВИЛЬНО. Утверждение путает цифры. В аудио говорится: '23 миллиона посетили Гатвик, и 2000 из них получили помощь'. Помощь получают только 2000 человек.

statements.statement: Daren Tylor’s problem is that he has lost the air ticket his mother booked for him.
statements.audio_transcript: One night he turned up here at Gatwick, after his mother told him she had booked him onto a return flight. When he got here, he found out that his mum hadn't booked him on the flight after all, so he'd been sleeping in the airport.

explanation.de: FALSCH. Darren hat keine Flugkarte verloren. Im Audio heißt es, seine Mutter habe ihm gesagt, sie hätte einen Rückflug gebucht, aber «sie hatte ihn doch nicht gebucht». Das Ticket existierte nie.
explanation.en: INCORRECT. Darren didn't lose a ticket. The audio says his mother told him she had booked a return flight, but 'his mum hadn't booked him on the flight after all'. The ticket never existed.
explanation.ar: خطأ. لم يفقد دارين تذكرة. يقول التسجيل إن أمه أخبرته أنها حجزت له رحلة عودة، لكن 'لم تكن قد حجزت له في الواقع'. التذكرة لم تكن موجودة أصلاً.
explanation.fr: FAUX. Darren n'a pas perdu de billet. L'audio dit que sa mère lui avait dit qu'elle avait réservé un vol retour, mais 'elle ne l'avait pas réservé en fait'. Le billet n'a jamais existé.
explanation.es: INCORRECTO. Darren no perdió un billete. El audio dice que su madre le dijo que había reservado un vuelo de regreso, pero 'en realidad no lo había reservado'. El billete nunca existió.
explanation.ru: НЕПРАВИЛЬНО. Даррен не терял билет. В аудио говорится, что мать сказала ему, что забронировала обратный рейс, но 'на самом деле не забронировала'. Билета никогда не существовало.

statements.audio_transcript: Like most Gatwick passengers, Darren had never heard of Travel Care. The service does not advertise its presence.

explanation.de: FALSCH. Im Audio heißt es, Darren habe «noch nie von Travel Care gehört» und der Dienst «wirbt nicht für seine Präsenz». Travel Care ist also nicht bekannt.
explanation.en: INCORRECT. The audio says Darren 'had never heard of Travel Care' and 'the service does not advertise its presence'. Travel Care is deliberately low-profile, not well-known.
explanation.ar: خطأ. يقول التسجيل إن دارين 'لم يسمع ب~ Travel Care من قبل' و'الخدمة لا تعلن عن وجودها'. إنها ليست معروفة.
explanation.fr: FAUX. L'audio dit que Darren 'n'avait jamais entendu parler de Travel Care' et que 'le service ne fait pas de publicité'. Travel Care n'est pas connu.
explanation.es: INCORRECTO. El audio dice que Darren 'nunca había oído hablar de Travel Care' y 'el servicio no anuncia su presencia'. Travel Care no es conocido.
explanation.ru: НЕПРАВИЛЬНО. В аудио говорится, что Даррен 'никогда не слышал о Travel Care' и 'служба не рекламирует себя'. Она не является известной.

statements.statement: Travel-Care never gives travellers the money to buy their tickets.
statements.audio_transcript: Some people do try, thinking we'll give them money on any old excuse, but I never give them the cash. I always go to the ticket office with them.

explanation.de: RICHTIG. Ruth sagt ausdrücklich: «Ich gebe ihnen nie das Bargeld. Ich gehe immer mit ihnen zum Fahrkartenschalter.» Travel Care kauft Tickets direkt, gibt aber kein Geld aus.
explanation.en: CORRECT. Ruth explicitly says 'I never give them the cash. I always go to the ticket office with them.' Travel Care buys tickets directly but never hands over money to travellers.
explanation.ar: صحيح. تقول روث صراحةً: 'لا أعطيهم النقود أبداً. أذهب دائماً معهم إلى مكتب التذاكر.' Travel Care يشتري التذاكر مباشرة لكن لا يسلم المال.
explanation.fr: CORRECT. Ruth dit explicitement : 'Je ne leur donne jamais l'argent. Je vais toujours au guichet avec eux.' Travel Care achète les billets directement mais ne donne jamais d'argent.
explanation.es: CORRECTO. Ruth dice explícitamente: 'Nunca les doy el dinero. Siempre voy con ellos a la taquilla.' Travel Care compra los billetes directamente pero nunca entrega dinero.
explanation.ru: ПРАВИЛЬНО. Рут прямо говорит: 'я никогда не даю им наличные. Я всегда иду с ними в кассу.' Travel Care покупает билеты напрямую, но никогда не отдаёт деньги.

statements.statement: According to the report, Heathrow and Gatwick are the only two airports in the world offering this kind of service.
statements.audio_transcript: Britain leads the world in emergency help for travellers. Apart from a similar organisation at London's other airport, Heathrow, no other airport in the world offers such a service.

explanation.de: RICHTIG. Im Audio heißt es: «Außer einer ähnlichen Organisation am anderen Londoner Flughafen Heathrow bietet kein anderer Flughafen der Welt einen solchen Service.» Also nur Gatwick und Heathrow.
explanation.en: CORRECT. The audio states 'apart from a similar organisation at London's other airport, Heathrow, no other airport in the world offers such a service'. Only Gatwick and Heathrow provide this.
explanation.ar: صحيح. يقول التسجيل: 'باستثناء منظمة مماثلة في مطار لندن الآخر هيثرو، لا يقدم أي مطار آخر في العالم هذه الخدمة.' فقط غاتويك وهيثرو.
explanation.fr: CORRECT. L'audio dit : 'À part une organisation similaire à l'autre aéroport de Londres, Heathrow, aucun autre aéroport au monde n'offre un tel service.' Seuls Gatwick et Heathrow le proposent.
explanation.es: CORRECTO. El audio dice: 'Aparte de una organización similar en el otro aeropuerto de Londres, Heathrow, ningún otro aeropuerto del mundo ofrece este servicio.' Solo Gatwick y Heathrow lo tienen.
explanation.ru: ПРАВИЛЬНО. В аудио говорится: 'Кроме аналогичной организации в другом лондонском аэропорту Хитроу, ни один аэропорт в мире не предлагает такой услуги.' Только Гатвик и Хитроу.

statements.statement: Many of the Travel-Care assistants used to work at the airport themselves.
statements.audio_transcript: This money pays for the three employees and twelve other assistants, most of them former airport staff, ranging from airport directors to duty-free shop assistants.

explanation.de: RICHTIG. Im Audio heißt es, die zwölf Assistenten seien «meist ehemalige Flughafenmitarbeiter, von Flughafendirektoren bis hin zu Duty-free-Verkäufern». Sie haben also selbst am Flughafen gearbeitet.
explanation.en: CORRECT. The audio says the twelve assistants are 'most of them former airport staff, ranging from airport directors to duty-free shop assistants'. They previously worked at the airport.
explanation.ar: صحيح. يقول التسجيل إن المساعدين الاثني عشر هم 'معظمهم موظفون سابقون في المطار، من مديري المطار إلى بائعي السوق الحرة'. عملوا سابقاً في المطار.
explanation.fr: CORRECT. L'audio dit que les douze assistants sont 'pour la plupart d'anciens employés de l'aéroport, allant de directeurs d'aéroport à des vendeurs de boutiques duty-free'. Ils travaillaient auparavant à l'aéroport.
explanation.es: CORRECTO. El audio dice que los doce asistentes son 'en su mayoría ex empleados del aeropuerto, desde directores de aeropuerto hasta dependientes de tiendas duty-free'. Trabajaban previamente en el aeropuerto.
explanation.ru: ПРАВИЛЬНО. В аудио говорится, что двенадцать помощников — 'большинство из них бывшие сотрудники аэропорта, от директоров до продавцов duty-free'. Они раньше работали в аэропорту.
